coredata.dc:title: A fluorescence and phosphorescence dual-signal readout platform based on carbon dots/SiO2 for multi-channel detections of carbaryl, thiram and chlorpyrifos
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Jing
Sun, Xiaobo
Pan, Wei
Wang, Jinping
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a fluorescence and phosphorescence dual-signal readout platform for multi-channel detections of carbaryl, thiram and chlorpyrifos was constructed by confining carbon dots (CDs) in SiO2 nanoparticles. The CDs were synthesized by a simple hydrothermal treatment of fresh pomegranate seeds, L-aspartic acid and L-tryptophan (PAT-CDs). PAT-CDs embedded in SiO2 nanoparticles (PAT-CDs@SiO2) emitted blue fluorescence and very long lifetime green phosphorescence in aqueous solution. Accordingly, one detection channel for carbaryl on the PAT-CDs@SiO2 platform was developed with a ratiometric approach applying dual-signal readouts from two emission wavelengths. Moreover, two detection channels for thiram and chlorpyrifos with a fluorescence and phosphorescence dual-signal readout strategy were built on the PAT-CDs@SiO2 platform. Finally, the proposed multi-channel detection method recovered carbaryl, thiram and chlorpyrifos in tap and pork samples in the range of 90% and 110%. Therefore, multi-channel detection functions can be potentially integrated onto CDs based platforms due to their high performances and possible multi-signal readouts.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual-signal readout platform
Multi-channel detection
Pesticide
Ratiometric method
Phosphorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detections of carbaryl, thiram, chlorpyrifos in aqueous solution
Detections of carbaryl, thiram and chlorpyrifos in real samples
Synthesis and characterization of PAT-CDs@ SiO2
One detection channel for carbaryl with a ratiometric approach applying dual-signal readout from two emission wavelengths
One detection channel for thiram with a fluorescence and phosphorescence dual-signal readout strategy assisted by Cu2+
One detection channel for chlorpyrifos in combination of a fluorescence and phosphorescence dual-signal readout strategy with the activity of AChE on ATCh
Detections of carbaryl, thiram and chlorpyrifos in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Key R&D Program of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Key Research and Development Program of China
China Science and Technology Exchange Center

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by National Key R&D Program of China ( 2017YFE0105200 ) and Postgraduate Innovation Program of Qingdao Agricultural University (QNYCX20060).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of PAT-CDs. (B) Particle size distribution of PAT-CDs. (C) TEM image of SiO2 (D) Particle size distribution of SiO2. (E) TEM image of PAT-CDs@SiO2. (F) Particle size distribution of PAT-CDs@SiO2.
(A) FT-IR spectra of the PAT-CDs, PAT-CDs@SiO2 and SiO2. (B) XRD patterns of the PAT-CDs and PAT-CDs@SiO2.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of PAT-CDs. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of PAT-CDs@SiO2. (C) Phosphorescence emission spectra of PAT-CDs@SiO2. (D) The absorption spectra of (a) PAT-CDs, (b) PAT-CDs@SiO2, (c) fluorescence excitation and (d) emission spectra of PAT-CDs@SiO2, and (e) phosphorescence excitation and (f) emission spectra of PAT-CDs@SiO2. (E) Fluorescence lifetime decay and (F) phosphorescence lifetime decay curve of the PAT-CDs@SiO2 collected at 450 nm and 480 nm, respectively.

(A)Fluorescence spectra of PAT-CD@SiO2 after adding different concentrations of chlorpyrifos. (B) Phosphorescence spectra of PAT-CD@SiO2 after adding different concentrations of chlorpyrifos. The fitting curves of (C) F/F0 and (D) P/P0 versus the concentration of chlorpyrifos.
Schematic illustration of a fluorescence and phosphorescence dual-signal readout platform based on PAT-CDs@SiO2 for multi-channel detections of carbaryl, thiram and chlorpyrifos.
Analytical results for the detection of non-spiked standard samples.
Analytical results for the detection of carbaryl, thiram and chlorpyrifos in tap water and pork samples.

head.ce:title: A fluorescence and phosphorescence dual-signal readout platform based on carbon dots/SiO2 for multi-channel detections of carbaryl, thiram and chlorpyrifos

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao 266109, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemical and Pharmaceutical Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemical and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao 266109, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a fluorescence and phosphorescence dual-signal readout platform for multi-channel detections of carbaryl, thiram and chlorpyrifos was constructed by confining carbon dots (CDs) in SiO2 nanoparticles. The CDs were synthesized by a simple hydrothermal treatment of fresh pomegranate seeds, L-aspartic acid and L-tryptophan (PAT-CDs). PAT-CDs embedded in SiO2 nanoparticles (PAT-CDs@SiO2) emitted blue fluorescence and very long lifetime green phosphorescence in aqueous solution. Accordingly, one detection channel for carbaryl on the PAT-CDs@SiO2 platform was developed with a ratiometric approach applying dual-signal readouts from two emission wavelengths. Moreover, two detection channels for thiram and chlorpyrifos with a fluorescence and phosphorescence dual-signal readout strategy were built on the PAT-CDs@SiO2 platform. Finally, the proposed multi-channel detection method recovered carbaryl, thiram and chlorpyrifos in tap and pork samples in the range of 90% and 110%. Therefore, multi-channel detection functions can be potentially integrated onto CDs based platforms due to their high performances and possible multi-signal readouts.

ce:section.ce:para: To integrate multi-channel detections onto one platform is very interesting because it can improve detection efficiency with facile operation, effective cost and labor saving. Recently, carbon dots (CDs) have exhibited high potentials to be such platforms because of their unique optical properties, such as intensive fluorescence emission [1,2], wide excitation range [3,4], multicolor emissions [5,6], excitation dependence [7] and possible afterglow emission [8]. In addition, easy fabrication [9,10], low toxicity [11,12], good biocompatibility [13,14], high stability [15–17], magnetic nature [18], etc. make CDs more attractive to be developed as multi-channel detections integrating platforms. For example, Li et al., [19] integrated multi-channel detections for Fe3+, Hg2+ and Pb2+ ions into one platform based on the CDs capped with COO−, according to that the selectivity of the CDs to the metal ions could be switched by different buffer solutions via photo induced electron transfer mechanism. Also, they realized multi-channel detections of Fe3+, Cr(VI) and AA on one CDs-based platform in aqueous solution with high sensitivity and selectivity via different mechanisms. As well, in combination of excitation dependent emission with different quenching mechanisms, multi-channel detections for methotrexate (MTX), rutin (RT) and quercetin (QCT) were achieved by a CDs based platform [20]. However, most of the CDs as platforms to integrate multi-channel detections generally applied single-wavelength fluorescence emissions, which may suffer from auto-fluorescence from biological samples and scattering light interference from environmental samples [21,22].

Ratiometric approaches applying dual-signal readouts from two emission wavelengths of CDs can reduce background interferences because the intensity ratio of dual-signal readouts as response signal hardly suffers from the fluctuations of experimental conditions [23,24]. On the other hand, CDs are always used to develop fluorimetric and colorimetric dual-signal readout detection methods, which has been proven to have advantages of minimizing the measurement errors and high efficiency over the single signal readout methods [25]. Moreover, afterglow CDs usually exhibit emission at longer wavelength after the removal of excitation sources [26], resulting in larger Stokes shift compared to prompt fluorescence, which can avoid interferences from excitation light and self-absorption [27]. The longer lifetime of afterglow allows to select emission applied in detection at an appropriate time gate so as to possibly avoid spectral interferences from system and light scattering [27,28]. Therefore, combining the above advantages, to develop a fluorescence and phosphorescence dual-signal readout platform based on CDs for multi-channel detections will be very interesting.

As is well known, pesticides are indispensable in agricultural production [29]. However, excessive and improper use of pesticides will cause a large amount of pesticide residues, and then do harm to the environment and human health [30,31]. Many traditional methods have been widely used in the detections of pesticide residues with high accuracy, including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [32], gas chromatography (GS) [33], mass spectrometry (MS) [34], GC/MS [35], surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) [36] etc.. However, these methods always are realized with expensive equipment performed by well-trained technicians in laboratory after complicated pre-treatments [37]. Therefore, rapid and cost effective analyses of pesticide residues are in particular need of integrating multi-channel detections on one CDs platform. To date, in addition to only a few other pesticides, including ziram [38], carbendazim [39], fluazinam [40], and thiabendazole [41], detection methods for pesticides established by using CDs as probes are mostly observed for organophosphorus pesticides (OPs) [42,43]. To our knowledge, there are some reports based on the phosphorescence of CDs for detections [44], such as tetracyclines detection [45], water detection [46], metal ions detection [47], etc. However, no reports on multi-channel detections for pesticides integrated onto one CDs-based platform have been published till now.

In this work, a fluorescence and phosphorescence dual-signal readout platform based on a CDs@SiO2 composite was developed for multi-channel detections of carbaryl, thiram and chlorpyrifos (see scheme 1 ). The CDs were synthesized through a simple hydrothermal treatment of fresh pomegranate seeds, L-aspartic acid and L-tryptophan (PAT-CDs). PAT-CDs were then confined in SiO2 nanoparticles to form a PAT-CDs@SiO2 composite. Besides blue fluorescence, PAT-CDs@SiO2 can emit green phosphorescence with a very long lifetime of 1.78 s in aqueous solution. Accordingly, one detection channel for carbaryl was developed on the PAT-CDs@SiO2 platform with a ratiometric approach applying dual-signal readouts from two emission wavelengths at 335 and 440 nm. Additionally, two detection channels for thiram and chlorpyrifos with a fluorescence and phosphorescence dual-signal readout strategy were integrated onto the PAT-CDs@SiO2 platform. Finally, the methods based on the PAT-CDs@SiO2 composite could recover carbaryl, thiram and chlorpyrifos in real samples in the range of 90% and 110%, suggesting it is a good fluorescence and phosphorescence dual-signal readout platform to realize multi-channel detections.

Fresh pomegranate seeds (0.5 g), L-tryptophan (0.4 g), and L-aspartic acid (0.25 g) were added to 10.0 mL of ultrapure water, and then transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene autoclave. After heated at 180 °C for 6 h, a yellow solution was obtained. When the solution was allowed to cool to the room temperature, it was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min, and filtered through 0.22 µm filter membrane to remove large particles. Then the filtrate was purified with a dialysis bag (MWCO∼1.0 kDa) and dialyzed for 12 h. Finally, the PAT-CDs was lyophilized into a fluffy yellow solid.

The synthesis of PAT-CDs@SiO2 was carried out based on the method in the literature with a minor modification[48,49]. First of all, 50 mL PAT-CDs (0.4 mg/mL) aqueous solution and 8 mL tetraethoxysilane (TEOs) was mixed evenly in a flask. Then NH3·H2O (2 mL) was added into the above solution, at the same time, the mixed solution was heated in a water bath at 100 ℃ with stirring for 6 h. After the reaction finished, it was filtered with a 0.22 µm membrane to obtain a clear and transparent solution.

MnO2 nanosheets were synthesized by a simple one-step method according to the literatures [50–52]. 0.01 M KMnO4 solution (9 mL) and 0.1 M morpholine ethanesulfonic acid (MES) solution (pH = 6.0, 12 mL) were mixed uniformly, and then ultrasonically dispersed for 5 min to obtain a solution of MnO2 nanosheets. Subsequently, the solution was washed and purified for three times with ultrapure water, and finally an aqueous solution of MnO2 nanosheets was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detections of carbaryl, thiram, chlorpyrifos in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: Carbaryl detection: Various concentrations of carbaryl and PAT-CDs@SiO2 were mixed in PB solution for 20 min and then the fluorescence spectra were collected at the excitation wavelength of 260 nm. The fluorescence ratios (R335/450) were applied to fit the linear relationship with the concentrations of carbaryl.

Thiram detection: First, different concentrations of thiram and Cu2+ (40 μM) were mixed together and incubated for 20 min. Then PAT-CDs@SiO2 was added to the thiram/Cu2+ system and incubated for 30 min. At last, the fluorescence spectra and phosphorescence spectra of the system were collected, respectively.

Chlorpyrifos detection: The detection of chlorpyrifos was also based on the fluorescence/phosphorescence dual-signal based on the PAT-CDs@SiO2 platform. First, various concentrations of the chlorpyrifos standard (100 μL) and acetylcholinesterase (AChE, 150 μL) were mixed together and incubated for 30 min at 37 °C. Then, 10 mM of acetylthiocholine (ATCh, 100 μL) and PB buffer solution (pH = 8.5, 0.01 M, 100 μL) were added and incubated for 20 min at 37 °C. After that, MnO2 nanosheets (150 μL) and PAT-CDs@SiO2 were introduced, and the solution was diluted to 1.0 mL. After incubated for 20 min, the fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra of the mixture were recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detections of carbaryl, thiram and chlorpyrifos in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to further verify the reliability of the fluorescence and phosphorescence dual-signal readout platform for multi-channel pesticides detections based on PAT-CDs@SiO2, three standards with known concentration were used to evaluate the method. The standards of carbaryl (100 μg/mL) and thiram (1000 μg/mL) were diluted with ultrapure water to 50.0 ng/mL and 5.00 μg/mL, respectively. But, to further eliminate the effect of organic solvent on the enzyme, the organic solvent in the chlorpyrifos standard (100 μg/mL) was firstly removed and dissolved in ultrapure water containing 5% ethanol. Then, it was diluted with ultrapure water containing 5% ethanol to 0.500 μg/mL. The specific detection steps were similar to the above experimental methods, referring to 2.4.

Furthermore, tap water and pork were selected as actual samples to evaluate the feasibility for the detection of carbaryl, thiram and chlorpyrifos using the present CDs-based platform. The tap water samples were obtained from our laboratory and the pork was purchased from a local market.

The tap water was treated with a 0.22 μm filter membrane. Then, the resulting tap water samples were added with carbaryl, thiram and chlorpyrifos at different concentrations according to different detection channels. The pork samples (1.00 g) were treated by the mixture of acetonitrile and water (volume ratio was 4:1). Then, the mixture was sonicated for 10 min after being vortexed for 5 min. Subsequently, the pork samples were centrifuged at 10,000 r/min for 10 min. Then the obtained pork extract was treated with a 0.22 μm filter membrane, and 8 mL of the supernatant was diluted with ultrapure water to 200 mL for the next step.

Three pesticides at different concentrations were added separately, and the proposed method was used for the detections. The specific detection steps were similar to the above experimental methods, referring to 2.4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of PAT-CDs@ SiO2
ce:section.ce:para: The morphologies and the sizes of PAT-CDs, SiO2 and PAT-CDs@SiO2 were characterized by TEM. In the TEM image (Fig. 1 A), PAT-CDs display a quasi-spherical shape with an average diameter of about 3.8 nm. While, the particle size of PAT-CDs@SiO2 increased to about 7.0 nm with an irregular polygonal shape as the pure SiO2 (Fig. 1E, F and C, D) obtained by the identical preparation procedures of PAT-CDs@SiO2, indicating that PAT-CDs were coated with an amorphous silica shell [48].

The functional groups on the surfaces of PAT-CDs, SiO2 and PAT-CDs@SiO2 were characterized by FT-IR. As illustrated in Fig. 2 A, the broad peak at 3400 cm−1 in the spectrum of PAT-CDs@SiO2 is attributed to the stretching vibration of O – H. Also, the band at 1632 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibration of C = O. Two peaks at 3500 cm−1 and 1107 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of Si-OH and Si-O-Si, respectively. Moreover, compared to the FT-IR spectrum of pure SiO2, there are characteristic vibration peaks of N-Si (804 cm−1) and C-Si (1677 cm−1) on the surface of PAT-CDs@SiO2, which further indicates that PAT-CDs were covered by silica with covalent bond connections [48]. Their crystal structures were characterized by XRD and the results are shown in Fig. 2B. Both PAT-CDs and PAT-CDs@SiO2 exhibit a peak at around 2θ = 23.1° corresponding to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon, which indicates that the crystalline structure of PAT-CDs were not affected during the generation of SiO2 on the surface of PAT-CDs.

The fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra of PAT-CDs and PAT-CDs@SiO2 were collected in solutions under excitation at various wavelengths. As shown in Fig. 3 A, there are two fluorescence emission bands at 350 nm and 440 nm for PAT-CDs under excitation at the wavelengths from 240 nm to 300 nm. As excitation wavelength is larger than 320 nm, the fluorescence emission shows an excitation-independent property with only one peak at 440 nm. It suggests that there are two emission centers in PAT-CDs, one is originated from carbon core and the other is from the surface states [2]. Fig. 3B indicates that PAT-CDs@SiO2 have a similar fluorescence property as PAT-CDs. And both of them could generate “naked eyes” detectable blue fluorescence under excitation by UV light (inserts in Fig. 3A and B). Significantly, PAT-CDs@SiO2 in aqueous solution generated “naked eyes” detectable green phosphorescence after turning off UV light (see insert in Fig. 3C), but naked PAT-CDs didn't. The phosphorescence spectra in Fig. 3C display two bands at 430 nm and 480 nm when excitation wavelength was less than 360 nm, otherwise, only one emission band at 480 nm. As shown in Fig. 3D, in the absorption spectra, there are two characteristic peaks at 280 nm and 350 nm for both PAT-CDs and PAT-CDs@SiO2, which are attributed to the π-π* transition of C = C in aromatic sp2 domain and the n-π* transition of C = O in PAT-CDs [53]. Obviously, n-π* transition of C = O of PAT-CDs is the main reason to produce fluorescence and phosphorescence emissions of PAT-CDs@SiO2 since the absorption band at 350 nm is overlapped with the excitation spectrum. Interestingly, PAT-CDs@SiO2 displayed a very long fluorescence lifetime of 17.37 ns and a very long phosphorescence lifetime of 1.78 s (Fig. 3 D and E), and the “naked eyes” detectable solution phosphorescence could last for as long as 30 s (Figure S1). Due to the protective effect of the SiO2 shell, PAT-CDs@SiO2 were isolated from the phosphorescence quencher such as oxygen to produce phosphorescence in aqueous solution, which offers a possibility to build a fluorescence and phosphorescence dual-signal readout platform for multi-channel detections.

The stability of PAT-CDs@SiO2 was evaluated by investigating the effects of ionic strength, pH, irradiation time, and potential interferences in aqueous solution including metal ions and small molecules, on their phosphorescence and fluorescence emissions. As shown in Figure S2A and B, after continuous irradiation under Xe lamp for two hours, the fluorescence and phosphorescence intensity of PAT-CDs@SiO2 remained nearly unchanged. Also, ionic strength exerted negligible influence on the fluorescence intensity and phosphorescence intensity of PAT-CDs@SiO2, which was tested in the NaCl solutions at different concentrations of 0, 25, 50, 100, 200, 400 mM, respectively (Figure S2C and D). However, pH values could influence the fluorescence intensity of PAT-CDs@SiO2. It is interesting that the fluorescence intensity at 350 nm was gradually increased while the intensity at 440 nm decreased with pH rising from 3.0 to 11.0. Nevertheless, the phosphorescence intensity of PAT-CDs@SiO2 exhibited negligible variation when pH value fluctuated in the range from 3.0 to 11.0 (see Figure S2E and F). Therefore, PAT-CDs@SiO2 is a potential platform for pH with a ratiometric approach applying dual-signal readouts from two fluorescence emission wavelengths. Furthermore, metal ions including Ag+, Al3+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Cr2+, K+, Li+, Mn2+, Na+, Ni2+, Zn2+, and other substance such as NaBF4, Glu, KI, Leu, Na2S2O3, NaNO2, NaNO3, NH4Cl, Na2B4O7 did not exert effects on the fluorescence and phosphorescence intensity of PAT-CDs@SiO2 except Cu2+ and Fe3+ with a little quenching effect (see Figure S3A-D). Therefore, it can be concluded that PAT-CDs@SiO2 as a fluorescence and phosphorescence dual-signal readout platform will not suffer from the effects of ionic strength, irradiation time and potential interferences.

ce:section.ce:section-title: One detection channel for carbaryl with a ratiometric approach applying dual-signal readout from two emission wavelengths
ce:section.ce:para: As can be seen from Figure S4A, carbaryl can emit concentration-dependent fluorescence at 335 nm under excitation at 260 nm. Upon addition of the PAT-CDs@SiO2 composite, its contribution to the fluorescence emission at 335 nm was very little (Figure S4B-E). Obviously, with the addition of carbaryl in the aqueous solution of PAT-CDs@SiO2, the fluorescence intensity at 335 nm increased gradually while at 440 nm remained unchanged (Figure S5A). Therefore, one detection channel for carbaryl can be constructed on the platform of PAT-CDs@SiO2 with a ratiometric approach applying dual-signal readout from the two emission wavelengths at 335 and 440 nm. The fluorescence at 335 nm was used as the response signal readout while at 440 nm was used as the reference signal readout. The ratio of the fluorescence intensity at 335 nm to that at 440 nm (R335/440) as the total response signal was applied to calibrate a linear relationship with the concentration of carbaryl. As shown in Figure S4F, when the concentration of PAT-CDs@SiO2 was 0.01 μg/mL, the sensitivity of the linear relationship between R335/440 and the concentration of carbaryl was the highest. However, the signal readout at 440 nm was not stable and varied with the addition of carbaryl. Therefore, 0.02 μg/mL was selected as the optimal concentration of PAT-CDs@SiO2 in the detection channel for carbaryl. Finally, a good linear relationship between R335/450 and the concentration of carbaryl was obtained in the range of 0.01–0.1 μg/mL. A regression equation of R335/440 = 0.953 + 47.59c was fitted with a linear regression coefficient (R2) of 0.994 (see Figure S4B) and a limit of detection (LOD) of 3.3 ng/mL.

In order to evaluate the selectivity of the detection channel for carbaryl with the ratiometric approach, several pesticides, including kresoxim-methyl, acetamiprid, hymexazol, glyphosate, metalaxyl, avermectin, imidacloprid, chlorpyrifos, thiram and carbendazim were selected as the possible interfering substances. Their effects on R335/440 of the mixture solution of PAT-CDs@SiO2 and carbaryl were investigated and shown in Figure S6. Negligible effects from these possible interference substances were exerted on R335/440 even though their concentrations were 5 times higher than carbaryl, indicating an excellent selectivity of the channel detection for carbaryl with a ratiometric approach.

ce:section.ce:section-title: One detection channel for thiram with a fluorescence and phosphorescence dual-signal readout strategy assisted by Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: Thiram can form a complex with Cu2+ (thiram-Cu(Ⅱ)) with a characteristic absorption peak at about 400 nm [54] which is overlapped the excitation, fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra of PAT-CDs@SiO2 (Figure S7A and B). Moreover, upon addition of different concentrations of thiram (1–10 μg/mL) in the presence of excessive Cu2+, the fluorescence and phosphorescence intensities decreased gradually (Figure S8 A and B), while the fluorescence and phosphorescence lifetimes of PAT-CDs@SiO2 fluctuated slightly (Figure S7C and D, and Table S1). Therefore, thiram-Cu(Ⅱ) quenched the fluorescence and phosphorescence of PAT-CDs@SiO2 via an inner filter effect (IFE).

Accordingly, one detection channel for thiram with a fluorescence and phosphorescence dual-signal readout strategy can be developed on the platform of PAT-CDs@SiO2. In the presence of excess Cu2+, the fluorescence and phosphorescence intensities of PAT-CDs@SiO2 decreased gradually upon addition of thiram from 0.0 to 10.0 μg/mL and the quenching efficiencies [55] on the fluorescence and phosphorescence were 89.2% and 48.6%, respectively. According to the fluorescence signal readout, a linear relationship was built between the relative fluorescence intensity (F/F0) and the concentration of thiram (F/F0 = -0.1289c + 0.8324, R2 = 0.974) with a LOD as low as 108 ng/mL (Figure. S8C). Meanwhile, by using the phosphorescence signal readout, a linear relationship was also established between the relative phosphorescence intensity (P/P0) and the concentration of thiram (P/P0 = -0.0354c + 0.97356, R2 = 0.983) with a LOD as low as 400 ng/mL (Figure. S7D).

In order to evaluate the selectivity of the detection channel for thiram with a fluorescence and phosphorescence dual-signal readout strategy, several pesticides, including carbaryl, glyphosate, imidacloprid, ziram, chlorpyrifos, abamectin, fludioxonil were selected as the possible interfering substances. Their effects on the fluorescence and phosphorescence of PAT-CDs@SiO2 were investigated and are shown in Figure S9A and B. Negligible effects from these possible interference substances were exerted on the fluorescence and phosphorescence intensities (except imidacloprid and ziram) even though their concentrations were 5 times higher than thiram, which is indicative of an excellent selectivity of this detection channel for thiram with a fluorescence and phosphorescence dual-signal readout strategy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: One detection channel for chlorpyrifos in combination of a fluorescence and phosphorescence dual-signal readout strategy with the activity of AChE on ATCh
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Figure S9A and B, there is a wide band ranging from 200 to 600 nm in the absorption spectrum of MnO2 nanosheets, which is overlapped with the excitation, fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra of PAT-CDs@SiO2. Therefore, MnO2 nanosheets can exert an IFE or Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) to the fluorescence and phosphorescence emissions of PAT-CDs@SiO2. Thus, upon addition of MnO2 nanosheets from 20 to 300 μL, the fluorescence and phosphorescence emissions of PAT-CDs@SiO2 both were gradually quenched with the quenching efficiencies of 69.7% and 70.5% (Figure S10C and D). However, the fluorescence and phosphorescence lifetimes of PAT-CDs@SiO2 decreased from 17.66 to 15.09 ns and 1.20 to 0.835 s as displayed in Figure S7E and F, and Table S2. Therefore, the quenching mechanism of MnO2 nanosheets to the emissions of PAT-CDs@SiO2 should be the combination of IFE and FRET. Since AChE can catalyze the hydrolysis of ATCh to yield thiocholine (TCh) with sulfhydryl group, which can reduce MnO2 to Mn2+ (equal 1), decompose MnO2 nanosheets and recover the fluorescence and phosphorescence emissions of PAT-CDs@SiO2(Figure S11A and B). Moreover, OPs (in this work, chlorpyrifos was selected) can inhibit the catalysis activity of AChE on the hydrolysis of ATCh to yield TCh, then block the decomposition of MnO2 nanosheets and finally confine the recovery of the fluorescence and phosphorescence emissions of PAT-CDs@SiO2 (Figure S11A and B). Accordingly, one detection channel for chlorpyrifos was developed on the platform of PAT-CDs@SiO2 with a fluorescence and phosphorescence dual-signal readout strategy in combination of its inhibition on the activity of AChE on ATCh and the subsequent reaction of TCh with MnO2 nanosheets.

The conditions of the detection channel for chlorpyrifos on the platform of PAT-CDs@SiO2 were optimized by investigating the effects of the volume of MnO2 nanosheets and the concentration of AChE on the sensitivity of the method. As shown in Figure S12A and B, when the volume of MnO2 nanosheets was 150 μL, the fluorescence and phosphorescence emissions of PAT-CDs@SiO2 were mostly quenched. With the volume of MnO2 nanosheets being larger than 150 μL, the fluorescence and phosphorescence emissions of PAT-CDs@SiO2 were not sensitive to the amount of MnO2 nanosheets. Therefore, 150 μL was selected as the optimal volume of MnO2 nanosheets. As shown in Figure S12C and D, when the concentration of AChE was 0.3 mg/mL, its activity reached the highest, hence it was selected as the optimal concentration of AChE. Under the optimized conditions, the relative fluorescence intensity (F/F0) and the concentration of chlorpyrifos in the range of 0.1–1.0 µg/mL exhibited a linear relationship (F/F0 = -0.4619c + 0.9611, R2 = 0.996) with a LOD as low as 45.8 ng/mL (Fig. 4 C). Meanwhile, according to the phosphorescence signal readout, a linear relationship was established between the relative phosphorescence intensity (P/P0) and the concentration of chlorpyrifos in the range of 0.025–1.0 µg/mL (P/P0 = -0.3065c + 0.9153, R2 = 0.992) with a LOD as low as 16.3 ng/mL (Fig. 4D).

In order to evaluate the selectivity of the detection channel for chlorpyrifos on the platform of PAT-CDs@SiO2 with the fluorescence and phosphorescence dual-signal readout strategy, several pesticides, including dimethocarb, fludioxonil, glyphosate, imidacloprid, ziram, abamectin, carbaryl were selected as the possible interfering substances. Their effects on the fluorescence and phosphorescence of PAT-CDs@SiO2 were investigated and are shown in Figure S13. Negligible effects from these possible interference substances were exerted on the fluorescence and phosphorescence intensities (except carbaryl) even though their concentrations were 5 times higher than chlorpyrifos, indicating an excellent selectivity of this detection channel for chlorpyrifos with a fluorescence and phosphorescence dual-signal readout strategy.

As discussed above, the selectivity of the platform for the detections of carbaryl, thiram and chlorpyrifos was very high when they were detected separately. However, in the presence of thiram and chlorpyrifos, the detection channel for carbaryl with the ratiometric approach will be interfered (Figure S14A). Carbaryl and thiram will exert effect on the detection channel for chlorpyrifos with the fluorescent method, but not with the phosphorescent method when the three pesticides coexist in one sample (Figure S14B). Interestingly, the platform showed high detection selectivity for thiram in the presence of carbaryl and chlorpyrifos for both the fluorescent and phosphorescent methods (Figure S14C).

A comparison between this method and the previous methods was performed in Table S3. The LOD and linear range of the present methods for the detections of carbaryl, thiram and chlorpyrifos are comparable or even better than those of the other methods previously reported.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detections of carbaryl, thiram and chlorpyrifos in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to verify the efficiency and accuracy of the developed method, standard samples had been analyzed. As shown in Table 1 , the recoveries for the detections of carbaryl, thiram and chlorpyrifos with the proposed methods are in the range of 105–110%, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) is less than 5.53%.

Besides, tap water and pork samples were used to evaluate the feasibility of the dual-signal readout platform for multi-channel detections of carbaryl, thiram, chlorpyrifos in real samples. The real samples were pretreated according to the procedures in detail in the experimental section and then spiked with carbaryl, thiram, chlorpyrifos at various concentrations. Thereafter, recovery tests were performed by using the proposed multi-channel detection method. As can be seen from Table 2 , the recoveries for the detection of carbaryl with the ratiometric fluorescent approach are in the range of 90.5–106%, and the RSD is less than 6.19%. The recoveries for the detection of thiram by means of the fluorescence and phosphorescence dual-signal readout strategy range from 91.5% to 109% and 97.8% to 106%, with RSDs being lower than 8.97% and 5.67%, and the recoveries for the detection of chlorpyrifos by the fluorescence and phosphorescence dual-signal readout strategy are in the range of 97.5% to 107% and 97.3% to 105%, with the RSD being lower than 5.02% and 4.56%. Obviously, PAT-CDs@SiO2 as a platform realized the multi-channel detections of carbaryl, thiram, chlorpyrifos with high accuracy.

The CDs from a simple hydrothermal treatment of fresh pomegranate seeds, L-aspartic acid and L-tryptophan confined in SiO2 nanoparticles can emit blue fluorescence and very long lifetime green phosphorescence in aqueous solution. Accordingly, one detection channel for carbaryl on the PAT-CDs@SiO2 platform can be developed with a ratiometric approach applying dual-signal readouts from two emission wavelengths. Additionally, two detection channels for thiram and chlorpyrifos with a fluorescence and phosphorescence dual-signal readout strategy can also be integrated onto the PAT-CDs/SiO2 platform. Finally, the proposed method can recover carbaryl, thiram and chlorpyrifos in tap and pork samples with high accuracy. Therefore, PAT-CDs/SiO2 can be developed as a fluorescence and phosphorescence dual-signal readout platform for multi-channel detections of carbaryl, thiram and chlorpyrifos.
Jing Wang: Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Xiaobo Sun: Investigation, Methodology. Wei Pan: Formal analysis. Jinping Wang: Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Key R&D Program of China (2017YFE0105200) and Postgraduate Innovation Program of Qingdao Agricultural University (QNYCX20060).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2022.107408.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
